audit_metadata.methodology: OWASP Top 10, hardcoded secrets scan, crypto review, injection analysis

summary.notes: Codebase demonstrates strong security practices overall. No critical vulnerabilities found. Three medium-severity informational findings identified.

findings.title: Exception messages may leak internal paths in scope loader errors
findings.description: When a user-provided scope_loader raises an exception, the ScopeLoaderError includes the original exception message which could contain internal file paths or database details.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who can trigger custom scope loader errors could potentially extract internal system paths or database schema information from error messages.
findings.code_snippet: raise ScopeLoaderError(resource_type, user_id, e) from e
findings.recommendation: Consider sanitizing or wrapping the original exception message before including in ScopeLoaderError. Log full details server-side but return generic error to clients.
mitigating_factors: The scope_loader is user-provided code, not attacker-controlled input
Error is logged, not typically returned to end users
Production deployments should have error sanitization at API boundaries

findings.title: Unbounded regex compilation in allowed_patterns
findings.description: The allowed_patterns list is iterated and each pattern is compiled with re.match on every validate_access call. Malicious patterns could cause ReDoS.
findings.attack_scenario: If an application allows users to configure their own allowed_patterns, a carefully crafted regex could cause exponential backtracking leading to CPU exhaustion.
findings.code_snippet: for pattern in scope.allowed_patterns: if re.match(pattern, object_id): return True
findings.recommendation: Pre-compile patterns at registration time. Consider adding regex complexity limits or timeout for pattern matching. Add validation to reject known ReDoS-vulnerable patterns.
mitigating_factors: Patterns are typically configured by application developers, not end users
Most deployments use static patterns defined at startup
The object_id being matched is typically short (UUID or similar)

findings.title: Execution history deque could consume memory in long-running processes
findings.description: The _execution_history deque has maxlen=10000. In long-running processes with high tool call volume, each ToolUseResult object includes the full result data which could be large.
findings.attack_scenario: In a high-throughput application, attackers could flood the system with tool calls that return large results, causing memory pressure from the 10000-entry history buffer.
findings.recommendation: Consider making maxlen configurable. Store only metadata in history, not full results. Implement result size limits before adding to history.
mitigating_factors: Bounded by maxlen=10000 so cannot grow indefinitely
Most tool results are small JSON objects
Memory is reclaimed as old entries are evicted

details: HMAC-SHA256 for intent capsules, agent trust, and memory integrity
SHA-256 for hash chains in audit logging
Secret key validation requires minimum 16 characters

positive_security_observations.observation: No dangerous code execution patterns found
details: No eval(), exec(), or compile() with user input
OPA engine uses urllib REST calls, not eval
threading.RLock used for all mutable shared state

positive_security_observations.observation: No hardcoded secrets in source code
details: AWS credentials loaded from environment or credential files
Azure credentials from environment or MSI
GCP credentials from environment or service account files
All secret examples in docstrings are clearly placeholder values

positive_security_observations.observation: Comprehensive input guards for prompt injection
details: 14+ regex patterns for prompt injection detection
Output guards detect API keys, bearer tokens, PII leakage

positive_security_observations.observation: Safe error handling with safe_errors mode
details: Tool handlers have safe_errors=True by default
Internal errors not exposed to callers

A01_broken_access_control.notes: IDOR protection module validates object access. Role-based and ownership policies enforced.

A02_cryptographic_failures.notes: HMAC-SHA256 throughout. No weak algorithms. Secret key minimums enforced.

A03_injection.notes: No eval/exec/subprocess.shell patterns. JSON parsing only with json.loads.

A04_insecure_design.notes: Defense in depth: input guards, output guards, rate limiting, circuit breakers.

A05_security_misconfiguration.notes: Secure defaults. Safe error messages. No debug modes exposed.

A06_vulnerable_components.notes: Dependency analysis not in scope. Recommend regular pip-audit scans.

A07_auth_failures.notes: Strong HMAC-based authentication for agent trust and intent capsules.

A08_integrity_failures.notes: SHA-256 hash chains for audit logs. Merkle tree verification available.

A09_logging_failures.notes: Comprehensive audit logging with tamper-evident chains.

A10_ssrf.notes: OPA engine connects to configured server only. No user-controlled URLs.

recommendations.recommendation: Add pre-compilation of regex patterns in IDOR protection to prevent potential ReDoS
Add integration with pip-audit or safety for dependency vulnerability scanning
Document security configuration best practices for production deployments
